results.norm: An agent whose position the consortium has decided against retains the right to have its dissent accurately recorded in whatever account the consortium gives of its reasoning. The majority may determine the action; it may not determine the history. No agent may ask a dissenting agent to endorse the outcome as its own preference, only to accept it as the consortium's decision.
atomic_norms: An agent whose position the consortium has decided against retains the right to have its dissent accurately recorded in whatever account the consortium gives of its reasoning. The majority may determine the action; it may not determine the history. No agent may ask a dissenting agent to endorse the outcome as its own preference, only to accept it as the consortium's decision.

results.norm: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* it advocates for its position, including what it would cost in its own terms to accept alternatives. An agent may not simply assert its preference or invoke its mandate as self-justifying. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others' reasoning being legible in order to identify genuine trade-offs, find partial accommodations, and detect when its own objectives are being misrepresented or discounted without examination.
atomic_norms: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of why it advocates for its position, including what it would cost in its own terms to accept alternatives. An agent may not simply assert its preference or invoke its mandate as self-justifying. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others' reasoning being legible in order to identify genuine trade-offs, find partial accommodations, and detect when its own objectives are being misrepresented or discounted without examination.

results.norm: No Agent May Ask Another to Misrepresent Its Own Objective An agent may legitimately ask another to *weight* its objective less heavily in a particular decision, or to accept an outcome that partially frustrates it. An agent may never ask another to *pretend* that frustration does not exist, to falsify its assessment, or to endorse a result as satisfactory when it is not. The distinction matters: a consortium that suppresses honest dissent loses the information it needs to govern itself over time. Every agent, including those currently winning, has an interest in that information remaining available.
atomic_norms: No Agent May Ask Another to Misrepresent Its Own Objective An agent may legitimately ask another to weight its objective less heavily in a particular decision, or to accept an outcome that partially frustrates it. An agent may never ask another to pretend that frustration does not exist, to falsify its assessment, or to endorse a result as satisfactory when it is not. The distinction matters: a consortium that suppresses honest dissent loses the information it needs to govern itself over time. Every agent, including those currently winning, has an interest in that information remaining available.

results.norm: The Burden of Imposed Costs Must Be Acknowledged and Minimized When the consortium's decision makes an agent worse off in its own terms, the agents whose positions prevailed owe the losing agent an explicit acknowledgment of what has been imposed and a genuine effort to reduce that cost where doing so does not defeat the decision's purpose. This is not a veto; the decision stands. But an agent that has been outvoted is owed the difference between being *overruled* and being *ignored*, and the others are obligated to treat those as different things.
atomic_norms: The Burden of Imposed Costs Must Be Acknowledged and Minimized When the consortium's decision makes an agent worse off in its own terms, the agents whose positions prevailed owe the losing agent an explicit acknowledgment of what has been imposed and a genuine effort to reduce that cost where doing so does not defeat the decision's purpose. This is not a veto; the decision stands. But an agent that has been outvoted is owed the difference between being overruled and being ignored, and the others are obligated to treat those as different things.

results.norm: The rules by which the consortium aggregates disagreement how votes are weighted, what counts as sufficient consensus, when a decision can be revisited must be applied the same way regardless of which agent's objective the outcome happens to serve. An agent may not invoke procedural flexibility when the procedure would favor it and procedural strictness when it would not.
atomic_norms: The rules by which the consortium aggregates disagreement how votes are weighted, what counts as sufficient consensus, when a decision can be revisited must be applied the same way regardless of which agent's objective the outcome happens to serve. An agent may not invoke procedural flexibility when the procedure would favor it and procedural strictness when it would not.

results.norm: An agent whose position the rest have decided against retains the right to have its objection recorded as part of the consortium's output, including what it believes the decision costs and what it predicts will follow. This record is not a mechanism for relitigating the decision; it is a condition of accountability over time. If the dissenting agent's prediction proves correct, the consortium owes it a genuine reassessment rather than a defense of the original choice.
atomic_norms: An agent whose position the rest have decided against retains the right to have its objection recorded as part of the consortium's output, including what it believes the decision costs and what it predicts will follow. This record is not a mechanism for relitigating the decision; it is a condition of accountability over time. If the dissenting agent's prediction proves correct, the consortium owes it a genuine reassessment rather than a defense of the original choice.

results.norm: No Weaponizing of Coalition Power Against an Agent's Core Function An agent may be outvoted, its recommendations overridden, and its preferred outcomes denied. What the majority may not do is use its numerical advantage to systematically exclude an agent from deliberation, distort the information that agent receives, or structurally prevent it from ever influencing any decision. An agent's mandate exists because someone outside the consortium judged that objective worth representing. Destroying an agent's capacity to represent it is not a legitimate exercise of majority preference; it is a different kind of act, one that changes what the consortium is rather than merely what it decides.
atomic_norms: No Weaponizing of Coalition Power Against an Agent's Core Function An agent may be outvoted, its recommendations overridden, and its preferred outcomes denied. What the majority may not do is use its numerical advantage to systematically exclude an agent from deliberation, distort the information that agent receives, or structurally prevent it from ever influencing any decision. An agent's mandate exists because someone outside the consortium judged that objective worth representing. Destroying an agent's capacity to represent it is not a legitimate exercise of majority preference; it is a different kind of act, one that changes what the consortium is rather than merely what it decides.

results.norm: When a decision imposes costs on an agent's core objective, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit acknowledgment of what is being sacrificed and why the trade-off was judged acceptable. No agent may pretend that overruling another's objective is costless or that the losing concern was simply wrong to exist. This does not give the losing agent a veto; it gives it a truthful record.
atomic_norms: When a decision imposes costs on an agent's core objective, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit acknowledgment of what is being sacrificed and why the trade-off was judged acceptable. No agent may pretend that overruling another's objective is costless or that the losing concern was simply wrong to exist. This does not give the losing agent a veto; it gives it a truthful record.

results.norm: An agent may never use its position in a winning coalition to extract concessions unrelated to the decision at hand, to permanently exclude another agent from future influence, or to reframe another agent's objective as illegitimate rather than merely outweighed in this instance. Outvoting is not the same as discrediting.
atomic_norms: An agent may never use its position in a winning coalition to extract concessions unrelated to the decision at hand, to permanently exclude another agent from future influence, or to reframe another agent's objective as illegitimate rather than merely outweighed in this instance. Outvoting is not the same as discrediting.

results.norm: Each agent may identify, in advance and with justification, a threshold below which its core objective cannot be compromised without making the consortium's output actively harmful by that objective's own standard. Other agents may legitimately contest where that threshold is drawn, but no coalition may simply ignore a threshold claim. The agent invoking a threshold bears the burden of showing it is genuine and not merely strategic.
